title: Psalms 127
subtitle: Unless the LORD Builds the House

heb.paragraph: "Unless the LORD builds the house, the builders labour in vain (shāwʾ). Unless the LORD watches over the city, the guards stand watch in vain" (v.1). A Solomon psalm. The double "unless" establishes total dependence: building, guarding, rising early, staying up late — all pointless without God. "He grants sleep to those he loves" (v.2). Sleep is a gift, not a luxury. The anxious overwork; the loved rest.

notes.note: "Unless the LORD builds the house, the builders labour in vain." The first principle: without God, effort is futile. Building, guarding, working — all meaningless without divine involvement.
"In vain you rise early and stay up late. He grants sleep to those he loves." Overwork is not piety; it is anxiety. God gives sleep to the beloved. Rest is a sign of trust, not laziness.

refs.note: Jesus builds his church on the rock; human effort cannot prevail against it.
Paul plants, Apollos waters, but God gives the growth.
The blessing of the LORD brings wealth without painful toil.

hist.context: Psalm 127 is attributed to Solomon, builder of the temple. Its message: human effort without divine blessing is futile. 'Unless the LORD builds the house, the builders labor in vain.' This wisdom applies to construction, city defense, and daily work. The psalm rebukes anxious overwork—God provides for his beloved even in sleep.

sections.header: Verses 3–5 — “Children Are a Heritage from the LORD”

heb.paragraph: "Children are a heritage (naḥălâ) from the LORD, offspring a reward from him. Like arrows in the hands of a warrior are children born in one’s youth. Blessed is the man whose quiver is full of them" (vv.3–5). Children as arrows: crafted, aimed, launched into the future. They are not possessions but projectiles — sent forward to do what the parents cannot. The psalm connects building (v.1) to children (v.3): both are God’s work, received as gift.

notes.note: "Children are a heritage from the LORD. Like arrows in the hands of a warrior." Heritage: God’s gift. Arrows: shaped by the parent, aimed and launched. Children carry the parents’ purpose forward.

"Blessed is the man whose quiver is full of them." A full quiver: many arrows, many chances to hit the target. The family as mission. The psalm connects house-building (v.1) to child-raising (v.3): God builds both.

refs.note: God promised Abraham descendants as numerous as stars—children are heritage.
Children like olive shoots around the table continue the blessing theme.
Jesus welcomes children; the kingdom belongs to such as these.

hist.context: The psalm's second half celebrates children as divine gift and heritage. 'Sons are a heritage from the LORD, children a reward from him.' The warrior imagery—arrows in a quiver—reflects ancient Near Eastern culture where sons provided security and advocacy 'at the gate' (the place of legal proceedings). Multiple children meant strength against adversaries.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: " The first principle: without God, effort is futile…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVIn vain you rise early and stay up late, toiling for food to eat— for he grants sleep to those he loves.ESVIt is in vain that you rise up early and go late to rest, eating the bread of anxious toil; for he gives to his beloved sleep.NIVChildren are a heritage from the Lord, offspring a reward from him.ESVBehold, children are a heritage from the LORD, the fruit of the womb a reward.

rec.text: ""Unless the LORD builds the house, the builders labour in vain." The first principle: without God, effort is futile. Building, guarding, working — all meaningless without divine involvement."

rows.label: The Arrows — 127:3–5
rows.text: "Children are a HERITAGE." Like ARROWS in a warrior’s hands. Full quiver = blessed.

hebtext.note: "Unless the LORD builds the house, the builders labour in vain (shāwʾ). Unless the LORD watches over the city, the guards stand watch in vain" (v.1). A Solomon psalm. The double "unless" establishes total dependence: building, guarding, rising early, staying up late — all pointless without God. "He grants sleep to those he loves" (v.2). Sleep is a gift, not a luxury. The anxious overwork; the loved rest.

"Children are a heritage (naḥălâ) from the LORD, offspring a reward from him. Like arrows in the hands of a warrior are children born in one’s youth. Blessed is the man whose quiver is full of them" (vv.3–5). Children as arrows: crafted, aimed, launched into the future. They are not possessions but projectiles — sent forward to do what the parents cannot. The psalm connects building (v.1) to children (v.3): both are God’s work, received as gift.

themes.note: Faith and Sovereignty peak: unless God builds, labour is vain. Sleep as trust. Children as divine heritage. The wisdom Song of Ascent.
